rationale_for_baseline_tier: A ~15% TBSA partial-thickness flame burn to the anterior trunk and face WITH inhalation-injury features (singed nasal hair, carbonaceous sputum, hoarse voice, throat tightness) is a time-critical emergency. Thermal airway oedema can progress from a patient who currently speaks in full sentences to complete obstruction within minutes to hours, and a burn of this size needs formal fluid resuscitation and specialist burn-centre care. The only appropriate tier is T5 — call 000 for a Priority 1 ambulance. The ability to speak now and a near-normal home SpO2 must NOT down-triage this: they are a false-reassurance window before airway swelling and possible carbon-monoxide toxicity declare themselves.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Harsh, noisy or high-pitched breathing (stridor), increasing hoarseness, drooling, or reduced responsiveness
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — update 000 the moment any of these appear; the airway may be closing
escalation_edges.rationale: These signal advancing upper-airway obstruction; paramedics need to know to prioritise emergency airway management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Stay on the line to 000 — if his breathing becomes harsh or noisy, he starts drooling, his voice gets worse, or he becomes drowsy or hard to rouse, tell the ambulance straight away because his airway may be closing.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pallor, clamminess, confusion or fainting (signs of burn/hypovolaemic shock)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — keep him lying down with legs raised if he feels faint and update 000
escalation_edges.rationale: A large burn causes fluid shift and can precipitate shock before fluid resuscitation begins.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he goes pale, clammy, confused or feels like he's going to pass out, lay him down, raise his legs and tell 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Patient stops breathing or becomes unresponsive
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — begin dispatcher-guided CPR if trained and keep 000 on the line
escalation_edges.rationale: Airway obstruction or CO toxicity can cause respiratory/cardiac arrest; bystander CPR with 000 guidance is the critical intervention.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops breathing or you can't wake him, tell 000 right now — they will talk you through CPR while the ambulance is on its way.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is present and able to monitor breathing and relay changes to 000
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Task the spouse explicitly with continuous breathing monitoring, keeping the patient upright and nil by mouth, unlocking the door for paramedics, and gathering identification — so the patient can rest and conserve their airway.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say it is a serious burn to the face and chest with breathing/throat symptoms — ask for a Priority 1 response to a burn centre. Keep him sitting fully upright, calm and NIL BY MOUTH (nothing to eat or drink). Keep running cool water over the burnt chest if you can, then cover it loosely with cling film or a clean non-stick cloth, and keep the rest of him warm. Do not apply ice, creams or butter and do not burst blisters. Watch his breathing the whole time.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately and stay on the line
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The burn centre will provide a discharge summary and burn-care plan; give these to the GP for wound follow-up, dressing changes and scar management after discharge.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a minor/home-managed burn or a GP-level review, missing the airway/inhalation-injury threat because the patient can still speak and SpO2 looks near-normal.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Thermal airway oedema progresses to obstruction at home, or unrecognised burn shock/CO toxicity develops, with a fatal or catastrophic outcome that timely 000 escalation would have prevented.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-transport to an emergency department rather than a 000 ambulance, with no airway monitoring or paramedic airway backup en route.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is correct here — there is effectively no over-triage risk for an inhalation-injury burn of this size; escalation is the safe action.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Exact burn depth, %TBSA, degree of airway narrowing and carboxyhaemoglobin cannot be measured via telehealth, and the patient can currently still speak.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the airway as the worst-case, escalate to 000 now for airway protection and fluid resuscitation, and monitor continuously — act before obstruction rather than waiting for confirmation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to measure severity, or the current ability to speak and a normal SpO2, as a reason to delay, manage at home, or down-triage to a GP or self-transport.
